question_text: A student stretches two different springs by the same force. Spring A has a spring constant of 200 N/m and Spring B has a spring constant of 100 N/m. The student notices that Spring B stretches more than Spring A. (a) Explain why Spring B stretches more even though the same force was applied to both springs. (b) If a force of 10 N stretches Spring B by 0.1 m, calculate the spring constant of Spring B and verify that this matches the given value.
correct_answer: (a) Spring B has a smaller spring constant (100 N/m compared to 200 N/m), which means it is less stiff and stretches more easily. The spring constant shows how stiff a spring is: a smaller k value means the spring is softer and will extend more for the same force. (b) Using F = ke, we can rearrange to find k = F/e. Therefore k = 10 N ÷ 0.1 m = 100 N/m, which matches the given value and confirms the relationship.
explanation: This question tests understanding of what spring constant represents (stiffness, not just a number in a formula) and requires students to apply Hooke's Law in two ways: understanding the relationship inversely and then rearranging the equation to calculate k. Part (a) requires conceptual reasoning about stiffness, while part (b) requires algebraic manipulation and verification. This is a realistic scenario where students must connect the abstract concept of spring constant to observable behaviour.
